docs.title: Cultivator
docs.text: Cultivator is a CI/CD automation tool for Terragrunt that runs plan and apply operations directly from Pull Requests - similar to what Digger/Atlantis do for OpenTofu/Terraform, but built specifically for Terragrunt workflows.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
While tools like Atlantis and Digger work great for OpenTofu/Terraform, they don't fully support Terragrunt's unique features:

  • Dependencies between modules (dependency blocks)
  • Run-all operations across multiple modules
  • Hierarchical configuration with terragrunt.hcl inheritance
  • Impact detection when parent configs change

Cultivator is built from the ground up to handle these Terragrunt-specific scenarios.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• PR-based workflows - Run terragrunt commands via PR comments
  • Smart change detection - Detects which modules are affected by changes
  • Dependency-aware execution - Respects Terragrunt dependencies and runs in correct order
  • Run-all support - Execute plans/applies across multiple modules
  • No separate server - Runs in your existing CI/CD (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, etc)
  • Locking mechanism - Prevents concurrent applies to the same module
  • Rich PR comments - Beautiful formatted outputs with plan summaries

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
• Getting Started - Installation and first steps
  • User Guide - How to use Cultivator
  • Architecture - How it works under the hood
  • Development - Contribute to the project

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
1. User comments on PR:
   "cultivator plan" or "cultivator apply"

2. Cultivator detects changed modules

3. Builds dependency graph

4. Executes operations respecting dependencies

5. Posts formatted results as PR comment

docs.title: Example Usage
docs.text:
# Comment on PR to run plan for affected modules
cultivator plan

# Run plan for all modules
cultivator plan --all

# Apply changes (requires approval in some configurations)
cultivator apply

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• Documentation: Check the User Guide
  • Issues: GitHub Issues
  • Discussions: GitHub Discussions

docs.title: License
docs.text:

Latest Version: 1.0.0 | Last Updated: February 2026

docs.title: Configuration Guide
docs.text: Complete reference for cultivator.yml configuration file.

docs.title: File Location
docs.text: Place cultivator.yml in the root of your repository.

docs.title: Top-level Structure
docs.text:
version: 1                # Required: Config version
projects: []              # Required: List of projects
settings: {}              # Optional: Global settings
hooks: {}                 # Optional: Custom hooks

docs.title: Projects
docs.text:
Define Terragrunt projects/directories to manage.

projects:
  - name: production              # Required: Unique project name
    dir: envs/prod                # Required: Directory path
    terragrunt_version: 0.55.0    # Optional: Terragrunt version
    terraform_version: 1.7.0      # Optional: Terraform version
    workflow: custom               # Optional: Workflow type (default, custom)
    auto_plan: true                # Optional: Auto-run plan on PR
    apply_requirements:            # Optional: Requirements for apply
      - approved                   # PR must be approved
      - mergeable                  # PR must be mergeable

docs.title: name (required)
docs.text: Unique identifier for the project.

docs.title: dir (required)
docs.text: Path to the directory containing Terragrunt files. Relative to repository root.

docs.title: terragrunt_version (optional)
docs.text: Specific Terragrunt version to use for this project.

docs.title: terraform_version (optional)
docs.text: Specific Terraform version to use for this project.

docs.title: workflow (optional)
docs.text:
• default: Standard plan/apply workflow
  • custom: Custom workflow defined in hooks

docs.title: auto_plan (optional)
docs.text: Override global auto_plan setting for this project.

docs.title: apply_requirements (optional)
docs.text: List of requirements that must be met before apply: - approved: PR must have at least one approval - mergeable: PR must be in mergeable state - status_checks: All status checks must pass

docs.title: Settings
docs.text:
Global settings that apply to all projects (unless overridden).

settings:
  auto_plan: true           # Automatically run plan on PR open/update
  lock_timeout: 10m         # Lock timeout duration
  parallel_plan: true       # Run plans in parallel when possible
  max_parallel: 5           # Maximum parallel operations
  require_approval: true    # Require approval before apply
  delete_plans: false       # Delete plan files after apply

docs.title: auto_plan (default: true)
docs.text: Automatically run terragrunt plan when PR is opened or updated.

docs.title: lock_timeout (default: 10m)
docs.text: How long to wait for a lock before timing out. Format: 5m, 1h, etc.

docs.title: parallel_plan (default: true)
docs.text: Run plan operations in parallel for independent modules.

docs.title: max_parallel (default: 5)
docs.text: Maximum number of parallel operations.

docs.title: require_approval (default: true)
docs.text: Require PR approval before allowing apply.

docs.title: delete_plans (default: false)
docs.text: Automatically delete plan files after successful apply.

docs.title: Hooks
docs.text:
Execute custom commands at different stages.

hooks:
  pre_plan:
    - echo "Starting plan..."
    - terragrunt validate-all

  post_plan:
    - echo "Plan completed"
    - ./scripts/notify-slack.sh

  pre_apply:
    - echo "Starting apply..."
    - ./scripts/backup-state.sh

  post_apply:
    - echo "Apply completed"
    - ./scripts/update-docs.sh

  on_error:
    - echo "Error occurred"
    - ./scripts/notify-error.sh

docs.title: Hook Types
docs.text:
• pre_plan: Before running plan
  • post_plan: After successful plan
  • pre_apply: Before running apply
  • post_apply: After successful apply
  • on_error: When any operation fails

docs.title: Complete Example
docs.text:
version: 1

projects:
  # Production environment
  - name: production
    dir: environments/prod
    terragrunt_version: 0.55.0
    terraform_version: 1.7.0
    apply_requirements:
      - approved
      - mergeable
      - status_checks
    auto_plan: true

  # Staging environment
  - name: staging
    dir: environments/staging
    terragrunt_version: 0.55.0
    terraform_version: 1.7.0
    apply_requirements:
      - approved
    auto_plan: true

  # Development environment
  - name: development
    dir: environments/dev
    terragrunt_version: 0.55.0
    terraform_version: 1.7.0
    auto_plan: true
    # No apply requirements for dev

settings:
  auto_plan: true
  lock_timeout: 15m
  parallel_plan: true
  max_parallel: 10
  require_approval: true
  delete_plans: false

hooks:
  pre_plan:
    - echo "=== Starting Terragrunt Plan ==="
    - terragrunt validate-all

  post_plan:
    - echo "=== Plan Completed ==="
    - ./scripts/analyze-costs.sh

  pre_apply:
    - echo "=== Starting Terragrunt Apply ==="
    - ./scripts/create-backup.sh
    - ./scripts/notify-team.sh "Apply starting"

  post_apply:
    - echo "=== Apply Completed ==="
    - ./scripts/update-documentation.sh
    - ./scripts/notify-team.sh "Apply completed successfully"

  on_error:
    - echo "=== Error Occurred ==="
    - ./scripts/notify-team.sh "Error in Cultivator"
    - ./scripts/create-incident.sh

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Cultivator supports these environment variables:

  • CULTIVATOR_CONFIG: Path to config file (default: cultivator.yml)
  • GITHUB_TOKEN: GitHub API token
  • CULTIVATOR_LOG_LEVEL: Log level (debug, info, warn, error)
  • CULTIVATOR_AUTO_PLAN: Override auto_plan setting (true/false)

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Validate your configuration:

cultivator validate --config cultivator.yml

docs.title: From Atlantis
docs.text:
# Atlantis atlantis.yaml
version: 3
projects:
  - dir: environments/prod
    terraform_version: v1.7.0

Converts to:

# Cultivator cultivator.yml
version: 1
projects:
  - name: prod
    dir: environments/prod
    terraform_version: 1.7.0

docs.title: From Digger
docs.text: Cultivator config is similar to Digger but adds Terragrunt-specific features.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text:
1. Start Simple: Begin with minimal config and add features as needed
  2. Use Projects: Separate different environments into projects
  3. Set Requirements: Use apply_requirements to enforce safety
  4. Add Hooks: Automate common tasks with hooks
  5. Version Control: Keep cultivator.yml in version control
  6. Test Changes: Test config changes in a dev environment first

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Review advanced features in the User Guide
  • Check Examples
  • Read Troubleshooting

docs.title: What is Cultivator?
docs.text: Cultivator is a CI/CD automation tool for Terragrunt that runs plan and apply operations directly from Pull Requests, similar to Atlantis or Digger.

docs.title: How is it different from Atlantis?
docs.text: Atlantis is designed for Terraform. Cultivator was built specifically for Terragrunt, supporting: - Terragrunt dependencies between modules - Run-all operations - Hierarchical configurations with inheritance - Impact detection when parent configs change

docs.title: Do I need a separate server?
docs.text: No! Cultivator runs in your existing CI/CD (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, etc). No separate infrastructure needed.

docs.title: Can I use Cultivator with existing GitHub Actions?
docs.text: Yes! Cultivator is designed to work alongside other workflows. Just add the Cultivator workflow to your repository.

docs.title: What versions of Terragrunt are supported?
docs.text: Cultivator supports Terragrunt 0.40.0+. For best results, use recent versions (1.0+).

docs.title: What about OpenTofu/Terraform version?
docs.text: Cultivator works with any OpenTofu or Terraform version that your Terragrunt version supports.

docs.title: How do I run a plan?
docs.text:
Comment on a PR: 

cultivator plan

docs.title: How do I apply changes?
docs.text:
Comment on a PR: 

cultivator apply

docs.title: Can I run all modules?
docs.text:
Yes: 

cultivator plan --all
cultivator apply --all

docs.title: What if a module fails?
docs.text: Cultivator will post the error in the PR comment. Fix the issue and comment again to retry.

docs.title: Is my OpenTofu/Terraform state safe?
docs.text: Yes. Cultivator requires proper AWS/Azure/GCP credentials in GitHub Secrets. Only authorized users can run operations.

docs.title: What about sensitive outputs?
docs.text: Cultivator automatically redacts sensitive data marked in Terragrunt outputs. Additionally, outputs marked as sensitive = true are hidden.

docs.title: How do comments get validated?
docs.text: GitHub webhook signatures are validated. Only authorized collaborators can trigger operations (configurable).

docs.title: Cultivator doesn't respond to my comment
docs.text:
• Check your workflow file is in .github/workflows/
  • Verify the repository has permissions for the workflow
  • Check that you're commenting on a PR (not an issue)

docs.title: Plan/Apply fails with module not found
docs.text:
• Verify your Terragrunt structure
  • Check file paths are relative to repo root
  • Ensure terragrunt.hcl files exist

docs.title: Lock timeout errors
docs.text:
• Check if another apply is in progress
  • Verify lock storage is accessible
  • Adjust lock_timeout in cultivator.yml if needed

docs.title: Sensitive data appears in PR comment
docs.text:
• Mark outputs as sensitive = true in Terraform
  • Update redaction patterns in configuration
  • Report the issue if it's a known sensitive pattern

docs.title: Can I customize the output format?
docs.text: Currently, formatting is fixed. Custom formatting is a planned feature.

docs.title: How do I integrate with other tools?
docs.text: Cultivator can be used alongside other GitHub Actions. It respects branch protection rules and requires reviews when configured.

docs.title: Can I run custom scripts?
docs.text: Not directly. You can pre/post process with GitHub Actions before calling Cultivator.

docs.title: How do I monitor Cultivator runs?
docs.text: Check GitHub Actions logs and PR comments. GitHub also provides workflow run history and analytics.

docs.title: Support
docs.text:
Still have questions?

  • Documentation: Check our full documentation
  • Issues: GitHub Issues
  • Discussions: GitHub Discussions

docs.title: Quick Start Guide
docs.text: This guide will help you get started with Cultivator in 5 minutes.

docs.title: Step 1: Prerequisites
docs.text: Ensure you have: - A GitHub repository with Terragrunt configuration - Terraform and Terragrunt installed locally (for testing) - GitHub Actions enabled

docs.title: Step 2: Add GitHub Workflow
docs.text:
Create .github/workflows/cultivator.yml in your repository:

name: Cultivator

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened]
  issue_comment:
    types: [created]

permissions:
  contents: read
  pull-requests: write

jobs:
  cultivator:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: |
      (github.event_name == 'pull_request') ||
      (github.event_name == 'issue_comment' && 
       github.event.issue.pull_request &&
       startsWith(github.event.comment.body, '/cultivator'))

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          ref: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }}
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Setup Terraform
        uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3
        with:
          terraform_version: 1.7.0

      - name: Setup Terragrunt
        run: |
          wget -q https://github.com/gruntwork-io/terragrunt/releases/download/v0.55.0/terragrunt_linux_amd64
          chmod +x terragrunt_linux_amd64
          sudo mv terragrunt_linux_amd64 /usr/local/bin/terragrunt

      - name: Configure AWS Credentials
        uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          aws-access-key-id: ${{ secrets.AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID }}
          aws-secret-access-key: ${{ secrets.AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY }}
          aws-region: us-east-1

      - name: Run Cultivator
        uses: cultivator-dev/cultivator-action@v1
        with:
          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

docs.title: Step 3: Configure Cultivator
docs.text:
Create cultivator.yml in your repository root:

version: 1

projects:
  - name: infrastructure
    dir: infrastructure/
    terragrunt_version: 0.55.0
    terraform_version: 1.7.0
    auto_plan: true
    apply_requirements:
      - approved

settings:
  auto_plan: true
  parallel_plan: true
  max_parallel: 5

docs.title: Step 4: Test It Out
docs.text:
1. Create a Pull Request that modifies a Terragrunt file
  2. Cultivator will automatically run plan and comment on your PR
  3. Review the plan output in the PR comment
  4. Apply changes by commenting /cultivator apply

docs.title: Available Commands
docs.text:
Comment these on your PR to trigger actions:

  • /cultivator plan - Run plan on changed modules
  • /cultivator apply - Apply changes
  • /cultivator plan-all - Plan all modules
  • /cultivator apply-all - Apply all modules
  • /cultivator unlock - Remove locks

docs.title: Example PR Workflow
docs.text:
1. Open PR with infrastructure changes
  2. Cultivator automatically runs plan
  3. Review the plan output in PR comments
  4. Get approval from team
  5. Comment /cultivator apply to apply changes
  6. Cultivator applies and comments with results
  7. Merge the PR

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Read the Configuration Guide
  • Check out Examples
  • Learn about advanced features in the User Guide

docs.title: Plan doesn't run automatically
docs.text:
• Check if auto_plan: true is set in cultivator.yml
  • Verify the GitHub workflow has correct permissions

docs.title: Apply fails
docs.text:
• Ensure PR is approved if apply_requirements: [approved] is set
  • Check AWS credentials are configured correctly
  • Verify Terragrunt/Terraform versions match

docs.title: Module not detected
docs.text:
• Ensure the changed file is in a directory with terragrunt.hcl
  • Check file extensions (.hcl, .tf, .tfvars)

docs.title: Need Help?
docs.text:
• Check the FAQ
  • Open an Issue
  • View Contributing Guide

docs.title: Architecture Design
docs.text: Understand how Cultivator works under the hood.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Cultivator consists of several key components that work together to provide automated Terragrunt workflows.

docs.title: 1. Event Handler
docs.text:
• Receives GitHub events (PR opened, comment created)
  • Validates webhook signatures
  • Routes to appropriate handlers

docs.title: 2. Change Detector
docs.text:
• Analyzes Git diffs between base and head branches
  • Identifies affected Terragrunt modules
  • Builds list of modules to process

docs.title: 3. Dependency Graph
docs.text:
• Parses Terragrunt configurations
  • Builds dependency graph between modules
  • Determines execution order

docs.title: 4. Executor
docs.text:
• Runs Terragrunt commands (plan, apply)
  • Manages locks to prevent concurrent operations
  • Captures output and errors

docs.title: 5. Formatter
docs.text:
• Formats Terragrunt output
  • Redacts sensitive information
  • Creates PR comments with results

docs.title: 6. GitHub Integration
docs.text:
• Posts comments on PRs
  • Updates commit statuses
  • Manages PR reviews

docs.title: Data Flow
docs.text:
GitHub Event
    ↓
Event Handler
    ↓
Change Detector → Git Analysis
    ↓
Dependency Graph → Terragrunt Config Parser
    ↓
Executor → Terragrunt Commands
    ↓
Formatter → Output Processing
    ↓
GitHub Integration → PR Comment

docs.title: Smart Change Detection
docs.text:
• Analyzes file changes in the PR
  • Determines which modules are affected
  • Respects Terragrunt include/exclude patterns

docs.title: Dependency-Aware Execution
docs.text:
• Parses dependency blocks in terragrunt.hcl
  • Builds execution graph
  • Runs modules in correct order

docs.title: Locking Mechanism
docs.text:
• Uses file-based locks in shared storage
  • Prevents concurrent applies
  • Configurable timeout and retry

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
• Redacts sensitive data from outputs
  • Validates GitHub webhook signatures
  • Respects IAM permissions

docs.title: Configuration Storage
docs.text:
Cultivator reads from cultivator.yml in the repository root:

version: 1
settings:
  auto_plan: true
  require_approval: false
  lock_timeout: 30m

See Configuration for full reference.

docs.title: External Modules
docs.text: Learn about Cultivator's support for external Terraform modules.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Cultivator can automatically download and manage external Terraform modules, whether they come from:

  • Public Registry: Terraform Registry, GitHub, HTTP sources
  • Private Registry: Custom registries, private repositories
  • Local Sources: Git repositories, S3, etc.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Define external modules in your Terragrunt configuration:

terraform {
  source = "github.com/example/module.git//path?ref=v1.0.0"
}

Or using the Terraform Registry:

terraform {
  source = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws"
  version = "~> 5.0"
}

docs.title: Module Preparation
docs.text:
Cultivator automatically prepares external modules before execution:

  1. Download: Fetches module source
  2. Cache: Stores for reuse
  3. Initialize: Runs terraform init
  4. Validate: Checks module syntax

docs.title: Execution Flow
docs.text:
Change Detection
    ↓
Module Parsing (discover external module sources)
    ↓
Dependency Resolution (build execution graph)
    ↓
Module Preparation (download, init, validate)
    ↓
Plan/Apply Execution

docs.title: Caching
docs.text:
External modules are cached to improve performance:

/tmp/cultivator-cache/
├── github.com/
│   └── example/
│       └── module.git/
├── registry.terraform.io/
│   └── terraform-aws-modules/
│       └── vpc/
│           └── aws/

Cache location: ${TMPDIR}/cultivator-cache or /tmp/cultivator-cache

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: For private modules, provide credentials:

docs.title: GitHub Modules
docs.text: export GITHUB_TOKEN=ghp_xxxxxxx

docs.title: Private Registry
docs.text: export TF_REGISTRY_AUTH_TOKEN_example_com=xxxxxxxxx

docs.title: AWS (via CodeArtifact)
docs.text:
# Configure AWS credentials in GitHub Secrets
AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID
AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY

docs.title: Module Not Found
docs.text:
Error: Invalid source address

The source address is invalid: ...

Solutions: - Check module source URL is correct - Verify network connectivity - Check authentication credentials

docs.title: Version Constraints
docs.text:
Error: Unsupported version constraint

Solutions: - Update Terraform to latest version - Check version constraint syntax - Consult version constraint docs

docs.title: Large Module Downloads
docs.text:
For very large modules, you may need to increase timeouts:

settings:
  executor_timeout: 10m

See Architecture for how modules are integrated.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Thank you for your interest in contributing to Cultivator! We welcome contributions of all kinds.

docs.title: Ways to Contribute
docs.text:
• Report bugs - Open an issue with details
  • Request features - Share your ideas for improvements
  • Write code - Submit pull requests
  • Improve docs - Fix typos, clarify documentation
  • Test - Help us test and find edge cases

docs.title: Code of Conduct
docs.text: We are committed to providing a welcoming and inclusive environment. Please read our Code of Conduct.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
1. Read the Development Guide
  2. Set up your local environment
  3. Pick an issue or feature to work on
  4. Make your changes
  5. Submit a pull request

docs.title: Pull Request Process
docs.text:
1. Fork the repository
  2. Create a feature branch from main
  3. Make your changes
  4. Write/update tests
  5. Update documentation if needed
  6. Ensure all checks pass
  7. Submit PR with clear description

docs.title: Pull Request Checklist
docs.text:
• Tests pass locally: make test
  • Linters pass: make lint
  • Code is formatted: go fmt ./...
  • Tests are included for new features
  • Documentation is updated
  • Commit messages are clear and descriptive

docs.title: Issues
docs.text:
Found a bug? Open an issue with:

  • Title: Clear and concise description
  • Description: Steps to reproduce, expected behavior, actual behavior
  • Environment: Go version, Terragrunt version, OS
  • Logs: Relevant error messages or logs

docs.title: Questions?
docs.text:
• Discussions: Ask questions in GitHub Discussions
  • Issues: Search existing issues before opening a new one
  • Docs: Check the documentation

docs.title: Recognition
docs.text:
We recognize all contributors in our CONTRIBUTORS file and in release notes.

Thank you for making Cultivator better!

docs.title: Project Status
docs.text: Core functionality implemented: - Dependency graph analysis with topological sorting - HCL parser for Terragrunt configurations - GitHub event handling and PR integration - Command orchestration (plan, apply, plan-all, apply-all) - Lock management for concurrent operations - Output formatting and plan summaries - Configuration validation - CI/CD integration via GitHub Actions

docs.title: 1. Graph Package (pkg/graph/)
docs.text:
Purpose: Dependency analysis and execution ordering

Features: - Build dependency graphs from Terragrunt modules - Topological sorting (dependencies first) - Circular dependency detection - Find affected modules when changes occur - Group modules for parallel execution

Example: 

graph := graph.NewGraph()
graph.AddDependency("app", "database")
graph.AddDependency("database", "vpc")

// Returns: [vpc, database, app]
sorted, _ := graph.TopologicalSort()

// Returns modules that depend on vpc
affected := graph.GetAffectedModules([]string{"vpc"})

docs.title: 2. Parser Package (pkg/parser/)
docs.text:
Purpose: Parse Terragrunt HCL files

Features: - Extract dependency blocks - Parse terraform source references - Find include configurations - Discover all modules in a directory tree

Example: 

parser := parser.NewParser()
config, _ := parser.ParseFile("path/to/terragrunt.hcl")

// Get all dependencies
deps, _ := parser.FindDependencies("module/path")

docs.title: 3. Detector Package (pkg/detector/)
docs.text:
Purpose: Detect changed files and modules

Features: - Git diff analysis between commits - Map changed files to Terragrunt modules - Find modules affected by dependency changes

Example: 

detector := detector.NewChangeDetector(baseSHA, headSHA, workingDir)
modules, _ := detector.DetectChangedModules()

docs.title: 4. Executor Package (pkg/executor/)
docs.text:
Purpose: Execute Terragrunt commands

Features: - Run plan, apply, init, validate - Support for run-all operations - Capture stdout/stderr - Handle exit codes

Example: 

executor := executor.NewExecutor(workingDir, stdout, stderr)
result, _ := executor.Plan(ctx, modulePath)

docs.title: 5. Events Package (pkg/events/)
docs.text:
Purpose: Parse GitHub webhook events

Features: - Detect event type (PR, comment, push) - Extract PR number, SHAs, repository info - Parse commands from comments (/cultivator plan) - Determine if auto-plan should run

docs.title: 6. Orchestrator Package (pkg/orchestrator/)
docs.text:
Purpose: Coordinate the entire workflow

Workflow: 1. Parse GitHub event 2. Load configuration 3. Authenticate with GitHub 4. Detect changed modules 5. Build dependency graph 6. Execute commands in order 7. Format and post results

docs.title: 7. Formatter Package (pkg/formatter/)
docs.text:
Purpose: Format outputs for PR comments

Features: - Parse Terraform plan summaries - Clean ANSI codes from output - Truncate long outputs - Format module lists

docs.title: Build and Test
docs.text:
# Install dependencies
make deps

# Build binary
make build

# Run tests
make test

# Run tests with coverage
make coverage

# Lint code
make lint

# Format code
make fmt

# Run all checks
make check

docs.title: Local Development
docs.text:
# Build
go build -o cultivator ./cmd/cultivator

# Run
./cultivator --help
./cultivator validate -c cultivator.yml
./cultivator version

docs.title: Testing with Docker
docs.text:
# Build image
docker build -t cultivator:dev .

# Run
docker run -v $(pwd):/workspace cultivator:dev validate

docs.title: Unit Tests
docs.text:
• Each package has *_test.go files
  • Test core logic in isolation
  • Mock external dependencies

docs.title: Integration Tests (TODO)
docs.text:
• Test full workflow end-to-end
  • Use test repositories
  • Verify GitHub integration

docs.title: Manual Testing
docs.text:
1. Create a test PR in a Terragrunt repo
  2. Trigger Cultivator via comment or event
  3. Verify plan/apply results
  4. Check PR comments

docs.title: Example cultivator.yml
docs.text:
version: 1

projects:
  - name: production
    dir: envs/prod
    terragrunt_version: 0.55.0
    terraform_version: 1.7.0
    apply_requirements:
      - approved
    auto_plan: true

settings:
  auto_plan: true
  parallel_plan: true
  max_parallel: 5
  lock_timeout: 10m

docs.title: Workflow File (.github/workflows/cultivator.yml)
docs.text:
name: Cultivator

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened]
  issue_comment:
    types: [created]

jobs:
  cultivator:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: cultivator-dev/cultivator-action@v1
        with:
          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

docs.title: Must-Have Features
docs.text:
1. Proper approval checking - Verify PR is approved before apply
  2. State locking backend - Use DynamoDB/S3 for distributed locks
  3. Improved error handling - Better error messages and recovery
  4. Plan file storage - Save plans and apply exactly what was planned

docs.title: Nice-to-Have Features
docs.text:
1. Cost estimation - Integrate with Infracost
  2. Drift detection - Detect infrastructure drift
  3. Policy as code - OPA/Sentinel integration
  4. Multi-CI support - GitLab CI, Azure DevOps, CircleCI
  5. Slack/Discord notifications
  6. RBAC - Role-based access control for environments

docs.title: Performance Improvements
docs.text:
1. Parallel execution - Run independent modules in parallel
  2. Caching - Cache Terraform/Terragrunt downloads
  3. Incremental plans - Only plan truly affected modules

docs.title: Adding a New Command
docs.text:
1. Create command function in pkg/cmd/root.go
  2. Implement logic (possibly in new package)
  3. Add tests
  4. Update documentation

docs.title: Adding GitHub Event Support
docs.text:
1. Update pkg/events/events.go
  2. Add parser for new event type
  3. Handle in orchestrator
  4. Test with sample event payload

docs.title: Improving Output Formatting
docs.text:
1. Update pkg/formatter/formatter.go
  2. Add new formatting functions
  3. Use in pkg/github/client.go
  4. Test with real Terragrunt output

docs.title: Tests Failing
docs.text:
# Run specific test
go test -v ./pkg/graph -run TestGraph_TopologicalSort

# Check for race conditions
go test -race ./...

docs.title: Build Issues
docs.text:
# Clean and rebuild
make clean
go mod tidy
make build

docs.title: GitHub Action Not Triggering
docs.text:
• Check workflow file syntax
  • Verify permissions are set correctly
  • Check event filters (PR types, comment patterns)

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: See Contributing Guide for: - Code style guidelines - PR process - Commit message format - Testing requirements

docs.title: Resources
docs.text:
• Terragrunt Documentation
  • GitHub Actions Documentation
  • Go Testing
  • Cobra CLI Framework

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Welcome to Cultivator! This section will guide you through installation, configuration, and your first Terragrunt plan.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• Go 1.25+ (for building from source)
  • Git (2.0+)
  • Docker (optional, for containerized deployment)
  • GitHub Account with repository access
  • Terragrunt (for your infrastructure)

docs.title: Choose Your Path
docs.text:
• Quickest Start: Use GitHub Action
  • Full Installation: Installation Guide
  • Configuration Details: Configuration Reference

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
1. Quick Start - Get running in 5 minutes
  2. Installation - Install Cultivator
  3. Configuration - Configure your repository

Need help? Check the FAQ or open an issue on GitHub.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Configure Cultivator to match your infrastructure and workflow needs.

docs.title: Configuration File
docs.text:
Create a cultivator.yml file in your repository root:

version: 1

settings:
  # Auto plan on PR events
  auto_plan: true

  # Require approval for apply operations
  require_approval: true

  # Lock timeout to prevent deadlocks
  lock_timeout: 30m

  # Run modules in parallel (experimental)
  parallel_plan: false
  max_parallel: 1

docs.title: auto_plan
docs.text:
• Type: Boolean
  • Default: true
  • Description: Automatically run plan when PR is opened or updated

docs.title: require_approval
docs.text:
• Type: Boolean
  • Default: true
  • Description: Require explicit approval comment before applying changes

docs.title: lock_timeout
docs.text:
• Type: Duration
  • Default: 30m
  • Valid values: 1m, 5m, 10m, 30m, 1h, etc.
  • Description: Maximum time to wait for lock before timing out

docs.title: parallel_plan
docs.text:
• Type: Boolean
  • Default: false
  • Description: Run modules in parallel (experimental, not recommended for interdependent modules)

docs.title: max_parallel
docs.text:
• Type: Integer
  • Default: 1
  • Description: Maximum number of modules to execute in parallel

docs.title: GitHub Secrets
docs.text: Configure these secrets in your GitHub repository settings:

docs.title: Required
docs.text: Secret Description GITHUB_TOKEN GitHub token (auto-provided by GitHub Actions)

docs.title: Optional (based on your infrastructure)
docs.text: Secret Description AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID AWS credentials AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY AWS credentials AZURE_CLIENT_ID Azure credentials AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET Azure credentials GCP_PROJECT_ID GCP project ID GCP_SA_KEY GCP service account key (JSON)

docs.title: Branch Protection Rules
docs.text:
Recommended settings for your main branch:

  1. Go to Settings → Branches → Add rule
  2. Branch name pattern: main
  3. Enable:
  4. Require pull request reviews before merging
  5. Dismiss stale pull request approvals
  6. Require status checks to pass (include Cultivator if desired)

docs.title: Permissions
docs.text:
Cultivator requires these GitHub permissions:

permissions:
  contents: read
  pull-requests: write
  statuses: write
  checks: write

docs.title: Filtering Runs
docs.text:
Run Cultivator only on specific path changes:

on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - 'infrastructure/**'
      - 'terragrunt/**'
      - 'cultivator.yml'
      - '.github/workflows/cultivator.yml'

docs.title: Environment Variables
docs.text:
Set environment variables in your workflow:

env:
  TF_VAR_environment: production
  TF_VAR_region: us-east-1
  TERRAGRUNT_DOWNLOAD: /tmp/terragrunt-cache

docs.title: OpenTofu or Terraform
docs.text:
Cultivator runs Terragrunt, which in turn calls either OpenTofu or Terraform. Choose the binary in your terragrunt.hcl:

terraform_binary = "tofu" # Use OpenTofu (default in our examples)
# terraform_binary = "terraform" # Use HashiCorp Terraform instead

Make sure the selected binary is installed in your CI environment.

docs.title: Custom modules path
docs.text:
settings:
  modules_path: ./infrastructure

docs.title: Ignore specific patterns
docs.text:
settings:
  ignore_patterns:
    - '**/.terraform'
    - '**/cache/**'

docs.title: Redaction patterns
docs.text:
settings:
  redaction:
    enabled: true
    patterns:
      - 'password'
      - 'secret'
      - 'token'
      - 'key'

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Quick Start - Get started in 5 minutes
  • User Guide - Available commands
  • Multi-Account Strategy - Deploy across multiple AWS accounts
  • Security Best Practices - Secure credential management with OIDC
  • GitHub Actions Docs

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: Install Cultivator in your repository using GitHub Actions.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• GitHub Organization/Repository with admin access
  • .github/workflows directory (will be created if doesn't exist)
  • Terragrunt v0.40.0+ (or latest)
  • Git (for repository management)

docs.title: Step 1: Create Workflow File
docs.text:
Create .github/workflows/cultivator.yml:

name: Cultivator

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened]
  issue_comment:
    types: [created]

permissions:
  contents: read
  pull-requests: write
  checks: write
  statuses: write

jobs:
  cultivator:
    if: |
      github.event_name == 'pull_request' ||
      (github.event_name == 'issue_comment' && github.event.issue.pull_request)

    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Run Cultivator
        uses: weyderfs/cultivator@v1
        with:
          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          terragrunt-version: 'latest'

docs.title: Step 2: Commit and Push
docs.text:
git add .github/workflows/cultivator.yml
git commit -m "Add Cultivator GitHub Action"
git push origin main

docs.title: Option 2: Docker
docs.text:
Run Cultivator in a container:

docker run -v $(pwd):/work \
  -e GITHUB_TOKEN=$GITHUB_TOKEN \
  -e GITHUB_REPOSITORY=owner/repo \
  weyderfs/cultivator:latest

docs.title: Option 3: Local Installation (Development)
docs.text:
# Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/Ops-Talks/cultivator.git
cd cultivator

# Build from source
go build -o cultivator ./cmd/cultivator

# Run
./cultivator --help

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Create cultivator.yml in your repository root:

version: 1

settings:
  # Automatically plan on PR open/update
  auto_plan: true

  # Require approval before apply
  require_approval: true

  # Lock timeout for concurrent applies
  lock_timeout: 30m

  # Parallel execution
  parallel_plan: false
  max_parallel: 1

See Configuration Reference for all options.

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
1. Open a Pull Request with infrastructure changes
  2. Comment on the PR: 
    cultivator plan
  3. Wait for Cultivator to respond with plan results

If you see a formatted comment with Terragrunt plan output, installation is successful!

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Quick Start - Run your first commands
  • Configuration - Customize your setup
  • User Guide - Learn available commands

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text: Get Cultivator running in your repository in 5 minutes.

docs.title: Step 1: Add GitHub Action
docs.text:
Create .github/workflows/cultivator.yml:

name: Cultivator

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize, reopened]
  issue_comment:
    types: [created]

jobs:
  cultivator:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      pull-requests: write
      checks: write
      statuses: write

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Run Cultivator
        uses: weyderfs/cultivator@v1
        with:
          github-token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
          terragrunt-version: 0.55.0

docs.title: Step 2: Commit and Push
docs.text:
git add .github/workflows/cultivator.yml
git commit -m "Add Cultivator workflow"
git push origin your-branch

docs.title: Step 3: Create a Pull Request
docs.text:
1. Go to GitHub
  2. Open a Pull Request with your changes
  3. Comment on the PR:
cultivator plan

docs.title: Step 4: Wait for Results
docs.text:
Cultivator will: 1. Detect changed modules 2. Run terragrunt plan on affected modules 3. Post results as a PR comment

That's it!

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Learn about available commands
  • Configure advanced settings
  • Set up locking and approvals

docs.title: Common Commands
docs.text:
# Plan affected modules
cultivator plan

# Plan all modules
cultivator plan --all

# Apply changes
cultivator apply

# Apply with auto-approve
cultivator apply --all

docs.title: User Guide
docs.text: Learn how to use Cultivator to automate your Terragrunt workflows.

docs.title: Sections
docs.text:
• Features - What Cultivator can do
  • Workflows - How to use Cultivator commands
  • Configuration - How to configure your setup

docs.title: Quick Overview
docs.text:
Cultivator runs Terragrunt operations from PR comments:

User comments → Cultivator detects → Executes with dependencies → Posts results

docs.title: Change Detection
docs.text: Cultivator automatically detects which modules are affected by changes in your PR.

docs.title: Dependency Awareness
docs.text: Operations respect Terragrunt dependencies and run modules in the correct order.

docs.title: Locking
docs.text: Prevents concurrent applies to the same module, ensuring safe deployments.

docs.title: Rich Output
docs.text: Results are formatted nicely in PR comments with plan summaries and status indicators.

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• Check Features for what you can do
  • Read Workflows for command details
  • See Configuration for setup options
  • Browse the FAQ

docs.title: Features
docs.text: Cultivator provides powerful features for automating Terragrunt workflows in GitHub.

docs.title: Smart Change Detection
docs.text:
Automatically detect which modules are affected by changes in your PR:

Your PR changes:
  - infrastructure/vpc/main.tf
  - infrastructure/vpc/variables.tf

Cultivator detects:
  - vpc module is affected
  - Any modules depending on vpc

Runs plan for: vpc + dependent modules

docs.title: Dependency-Aware Execution
docs.text:
Respects Terragrunt module dependencies and executes in the correct order:

# infrastructure/app/terragrunt.hcl
dependency "vpc" {
  config_path = "../vpc"
}

dependency "database" {
  config_path = "../database"
}

Cultivator will run: 1. VPC (no dependencies) 2. Database (depends on VPC) 3. App (depends on VPC and Database)

docs.title: Locking Mechanism
docs.text:
Prevents concurrent apply operations to the same module:

  • File-based locking
  • Configurable timeout
  • Automatic cleanup on completion
  • Prevents mistakes during simultaneous deployments

docs.title: Rich PR Comments
docs.text:
Formatted output with:

  • Plan summary (add/change/destroy counts)
  • Affected modules list
  • Dependency graph visualization
  • Status indicators
  • Error messages with troubleshooting tips

Example PR comment:

Plan Results for VPC Module

Status: Changes Detected

Resources:
  Add:       3
  Change:    1
  Destroy:   0

Modules: vpc, vpc-security-groups

Applied: ✓ Run with: cultivator apply

docs.title: Redaction of Sensitive Data
docs.text: Automatically redacts: - AWS access keys - Password fields - API tokens - Database credentials - Custom patterns

docs.title: GitHub Webhook Validation
docs.text:
• Validates webhook signatures
  • Prevents unauthorized requests
  • Respects GitHub permissions

docs.title: IAM Integration
docs.text:
• Requires proper cloud credentials in GitHub Secrets
  • Respects IAM policies
  • Logs all operations for audit

docs.title: Parallel Execution (Experimental)
docs.text:
Run independent modules in parallel for faster execution:

settings:
  parallel_plan: true
  max_parallel: 4

Warning: Only use for modules without dependencies.

docs.title: Cross-Platform Support
docs.text:
Works with: - GitHub Actions (recommended) - GitLab CI - Jenkins - Other CI/CD platforms

Docker container for flexibility.

docs.title: Git Integration
docs.text:
• Detects merge-base automatically
  • Works with any branch strategy
  • Respects .gitignore patterns
  • Handles large repositories efficiently

docs.title: Module Support
docs.text:
• Terragrunt: Full support for all features
  • Terraform: Works with Terragrunt-wrapped Terraform
  • LocalStack: Local testing support
  • Both: Works in hybrid environments

docs.title: Approval Workflows
docs.text:
• Require approval for apply operations
  • Comment-based approval
  • Integration with GitHub branch protection
  • Customizable approval policies

docs.title: Monitoring & Logging
docs.text:
• GitHub Actions logs
  • PR comment history
  • Detailed error messages
  • Audit trail of all operations

See User Guide for how to use these features.

docs.title: GitLab Pipelines Integration
docs.text:
This guide shows how to integrate Cultivator with GitLab CI/CD pipelines.

Note: By default, this configuration uses OpenTofu, an open-source fork of Terraform that maintains full compatibility. If you prefer HashiCorp Terraform, simply set IAC_TOOL: "terraform" in the variables section.

docs.title: Option 1: Using Docker Image (Recommended)
docs.text: The easiest way to use Cultivator in GitLab CI is via Docker image.

docs.title: Step 1: Create .gitlab-ci.yml
docs.text:
Create .gitlab-ci.yml in your repository root:

stages:
  - plan
  - apply

variables:
  OPENTOFU_VERSION: "1.7.0"
  TERRAGRUNT_VERSION: "0.55.0"
  # Set to 'terraform' if you prefer HashiCorp Terraform instead of OpenTofu
  IAC_TOOL: "opentofu"

workflow:
  rules:
    - if: '$CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE == "merge_request_event"'
    - if: '$CI_COMMIT_BRANCH && $CI_OPEN_MRS'
      when: never
    - if: '$CI_COMMIT_BRANCH'

cultivator_plan:
  stage: plan
  image: ubuntu:latest
  rules:
    - if: '$CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE == "merge_request_event"'
  before_script:
    - apt-get update && apt-get install -y wget unzip git curl
    - |
      if [ "$IAC_TOOL" = "terraform" ]; then
        wget -q https://releases.hashicorp.com/terraform/${OPENTOFU_VERSION}/terraform_${OPENTOFU_VERSION}_linux_amd64.zip
        unzip -q terraform_${OPENTOFU_VERSION}_linux_amd64.zip -d /usr/local/bin && rm terraform_${OPENTOFU_VERSION}_linux_amd64.zip
      else
        wget -q https://github.com/opentofu/opentofu/releases/download/v${OPENTOFU_VERSION}/tofu_${OPENTOFU_VERSION}_linux_amd64.zip
        unzip -q tofu_${OPENTOFU_VERSION}_linux_amd64.zip -d /usr/local/bin && rm tofu_${OPENTOFU_VERSION}_linux_amd64.zip
      fi
    - wget -q https://github.com/gruntwork-io/terragrunt/releases/download/v${TERRAGRUNT_VERSION}/terragrunt_linux_amd64
    - chmod +x terragrunt_linux_amd64 && mv terragrunt_linux_amd64 /usr/local/bin/terragrunt
  script:
    - terragrunt run-all plan --terragrunt-non-interactive
  artifacts:
    paths:
      - .terraform/
      - "**/*.tfplan"
    expire_in: 1 day
  only:
    - merge_requests

cultivator_apply:
  stage: apply
  image: ubuntu:latest
  rules:
    - if: '$CI_PIPELINE_SOURCE == "merge_request_event" && $CI_MERGE_REQUEST_APPROVED == "true"'
      when: manual
  before_script:
    - apt-get update && apt-get install -y wget unzip git curl
    - |
      if [ "$IAC_TOOL" = "terraform" ]; then
        wget -q https://releases.hashicorp.com/terraform/${OPENTOFU_VERSION}/terraform_${OPENTOFU_VERSION}_linux_amd64.zip
        unzip -q terraform_${OPENTOFU_VERSION}_linux_amd64.zip -d /usr/local/bin && rm terraform_${OPENTOFU_VERSION}_linux_amd64.zip
      else
        wget -q https://github.com/opentofu/opentofu/releases/download/v${OPENTOFU_VERSION}/tofu_${OPENTOFU_VERSION}_linux_amd64.zip
        unzip -q tofu_${OPENTOFU_VERSION}_linux_amd64.zip -d /usr/local/bin && rm tofu_${OPENTOFU_VERSION}_linux_amd64.zip
      fi
    - wget -q https://github.com/gruntwork-io/terragrunt/releases/download/v${TERRAGRUNT_VERSION}/terragrunt_linux_amd64
    - chmod +x terragrunt_linux_amd64 && mv terragrunt_linux_amd64 /usr/local/bin/terragrunt
  script:
    - terragrunt run-all apply --terragrunt-non-interactive
  only:
    - merge_requests
  dependencies:
    - cultivator_plan

docs.title: Step 2: Configure Project Settings
docs.text:
1. Go to Settings → CI/CD → Variables
  2. Add required environment variables:
  3. AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID (if using AWS)
  4. AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY (if using AWS)
  5. AZURE_CLIENT_ID (if using Azure)
  6. AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET (if using Azure)
  7. GCP_PROJECT_ID (if using GCP)
  8. GCP_SA_KEY (if using GCP)

docs.title: Step 3: Configure Merge Request Rules
docs.text:
1. Go to Settings → Merge requests
  2. Enable Require approval on merge requests
  3. Set Approval rules if needed

docs.title: Option 2: Using Cultivator Docker Container
docs.text:
If you want to use pre-built Cultivator image:

stages:
  - plan
  - apply

cultivator_plan:
  stage: plan
  image: cultivator:latest
  script:
    - cultivator plan
  artifacts:
    reports:
      dotenv: build.env
  only:
    - merge_requests

cultivator_apply:
  stage: apply
  image: cultivator:latest
  script:
    - cultivator apply
  when: manual
  only:
    - merge_requests

docs.title: Variables
docs.text:
Common GitLab CI variables you might use:

Variable Description CI_COMMIT_SHA Commit SHA CI_COMMIT_BRANCH Branch name CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID MR number CI_MERGE_REQUEST_APPROVED MR approval status CI_PROJECT_PATH Project path CI_PIPELINE_ID Pipeline ID

docs.title: Caching OpenTofu/Terraform and Terragrunt
docs.text:
cache:
  paths:
    - .terraform/
    - .terragrunt-cache/
    - .cache/

docs.title: Storing Plan Output
docs.text:
artifacts:
  paths:
    - "**/*.tfplan"
    - "**/*.json"
  expire_in: 7 days
  reports:
    dotenv: plan.env

docs.title: Manual Pipeline Triggers
docs.text:
To trigger Cultivator manually on demand:

cultivator_manual:
  stage: plan
  script:
    - cultivator plan
  when: manual
  only:
    - merge_requests

docs.title: Conditional Execution
docs.text:
Run Cultivator only on specific folder changes:

cultivator_plan:
  stage: plan
  script:
    - cultivator plan
  only:
    - merge_requests
  changes:
    - infrastructure/**/*.tf
    - infrastructure/**/*.hcl
    - terragrunt.hcl

docs.title: GitLab Merge Request Integration
docs.text:
Cultivator will automatically post plan/apply results as MR comments:

script:
  - |
    PLAN_OUTPUT=$(terragrunt run-all plan --terragrunt-non-interactive 2>&1)
    curl -X POST \
      "https://gitlab.com/api/v4/projects/$CI_PROJECT_ID/merge_requests/$CI_MERGE_REQUEST_IID/notes" \
      -H "PRIVATE-TOKEN: $CI_JOB_TOKEN" \
      -d "body=$PLAN_OUTPUT"

docs.title: Pipeline doesn't trigger
docs.text:
• Check workflow rules in .gitlab-ci.yml
  • Verify branch is not protected from CI
  • Check Settings → CI/CD → Pipeline for errors

docs.title: Permission denied errors
docs.text:
• Verify Runner has access to AWS/Azure/GCP credentials
  • Check Settings → CI/CD → Variables for correct secrets
  • Ensure service account has required permissions

docs.title: OpenTofu/Terraform not found
docs.text:
• Verify before_script is running correctly
  • Check Runner logs for installation errors
  • Try using Docker image with pre-installed tools

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Workflows - Learn about Cultivator commands
  • Configuration - Configure Cultivator behavior
  • GitLab CI Docs - GitLab CI/CD documentation

docs.title: Workflows
docs.text: Learn about the available Cultivator commands and workflows.

docs.title: Plan Command
docs.text:
Run OpenTofu/Terraform via Terragrunt plan on affected modules.

cultivator plan

Behavior: - Detects changed modules - Builds dependency graph - Runs plan in dependency order - Posts results to PR

Options: 

cultivator plan --all          # Plan all modules (not just affected)
cultivator plan --module vpc   # Plan specific module

docs.title: Apply Command
docs.text:
Apply approved OpenTofu/Terraform via Terragrunt changes.

cultivator apply

Behavior: - Requires approval (if configured) - Runs apply in dependency order - Locks modules during apply - Posts results to PR

Requirements: - Plan must have been run first - Must have necessary permissions - Branch protection rules respected

Options: 

cultivator apply --all         # Apply all modules
cultivator apply --force       # Skip approval requirement (if authorized)

docs.title: Basic Workflow
docs.text:
1. Open a Pull Request with infrastructure changes
  2. Cultivator automatically runs plan (if auto_plan: true)
  3. Review plan results in PR comment
  4. Comment: cultivator apply
  5. Cultivator executes apply
  6. Results posted to PR

docs.title: Approval Workflow
docs.text:
1. Create PR with changes
  2. Comment: cultivator plan
  3. Review plan output
  4. If changes look good: cultivator apply
  5. If require_approval: true, another reviewer must approve
  6. Apply executes and results appear in PR

docs.title: Multi-Module Workflow
docs.text:
Infrastructure structure: 

vpc/
  - main.tf
security-groups/
  - main.tf (depends on vpc)
app/
  - main.tf (depends on vpc and security-groups)

PR changes vpc/main.tf:

  1. Cultivator detects: vpc affected
  2. Determines: security-groups and app also affected (due to dependencies)
  3. Plans: vpc → security-groups → app
  4. Shows dependency chain in PR comment

docs.title: Status Indicators
docs.text:
Cultivator uses status indicators in PR comments:

Status Meaning ✓ Plan Complete Plan succeeded, ready to apply ✗ Plan Failed Plan had errors, needs fixes ⊗ Apply In Progress Currently applying changes ✓ Applied Apply succeeded ✗ Apply Failed Apply had errors

docs.title: Error Handling
docs.text:
If a command fails, Cultivator will:

  1. Post error details to PR comment
  2. Suggest troubleshooting steps
  3. Provide error context and logs
  4. Maintain lock (if apply failed)

Fix the issue and comment again to retry:

cultivator plan

docs.title: Concurrency & Locking
docs.text:
• Only one apply per module at a time
  • Lock timeout: configurable (default 30 minutes)
  • Automatic cleanup on completion
  • Prevents accidental concurrent applies

docs.title: Rate Limiting
docs.text:
GitHub API has rate limits. For large infrastructure:

  • Stagger multiple runs (don't run all at once)
  • Combine PR changes to reduce plan runs
  • Plan all modules, not frequently

See Features for more about what Cultivator can do.
